paper.Title: High Performance Rearrangement and Multiplication Routines for Sparse Tensor Arithmetic

fields.Research type: Algorithm development and performance evaluation for sparse tensor arithmetic
fields.By process phases, stages: Design of data structures, sorting/permutation algorithms, multiplication poly-algorithm
fields.By technology, framework, or platform: C++ libraries (LibNT, NTToolbox) and MATLAB Tensor Toolbox
fields.By application field and/or industry dom: Scientific computing, high-order differential operators, deep learning, numerical linear algebra
fields.Gaps and challenges: Lack of mature sparse tensor data structures, efficient sorting, handling hyper‑sparsity in tensor products
fields.Findings in research: LCO structure offers faster, memory‑efficient sorting; radix‑based permutation yields significant speedups; poly‑algorithm gives 40%+ gains and >10× speedup vs MATLAB
fields.Evaluation method: Benchmark experiments on high‑order differential operator cases, comparing against alternative high‑performance implementations and MATLAB Tensor Toolbox

paper.Title: A Creativity Survey of Parallel Sorting Algorithm

fields.Research type: Survey and classification study of parallel sorting algorithms across CPU, GPU, and other platforms.
fields.By process phases, stages: Classifies research objects, methods, and measurement techniques; outlines research problems, methods, and experimental analysis stages.
fields.By technology, framework, or platform: Focuses on CPU, GPU, and other platforms (FPGA, hybrid) for parallel MergeSort, RadixSort, and other sorts.
fields.By application field and/or industry dom: Applies to data mining, supercomputing, information systems, and general computing where high‑performance sorting is needed.
fields.Gaps and challenges: Load imbalance, thread contention, memory access conflicts, limited exploration of novel hardware platforms, and need for scalable parallel optimization.
fields.Findings in research: Provides a taxonomy of parallel sorting research, summarizes existing work, and identifies under‑researched areas and future innovation directions.
fields.Evaluation method: Systematic literature review with comparative analysis of experimental results reported in cited studies.

paper.Title: A Novel Hybrid Quicksort Algorithm Vectorized using AVX-512 on Intel Skylake

fields.Research type: Experimental performance study of a vectorized hybrid Quicksort implementation.
fields.By process phases, stages: Algorithm design, SIMD vectorization, implementation, and benchmarking.
fields.By technology, framework, or platform: AVX-512 SIMD extensions on Intel Xeon Skylake, C++ library integration.
fields.By application field and/or industry dom: General-purpose sorting for databases, image rendering, and high‑performance computing.
fields.Gaps and challenges: Efficient vectorized partitioning, handling small arrays, and adapting classic algorithms to AVX-512.
fields.Findings in research: Hybrid AVX-512 Quicksort achieves up to 4× speedup over GNU sort and 1.4× over Intel IPP.
fields.Evaluation method: Empirical benchmarks on Skylake CPU comparing runtime against GNU C++ sort and Intel IPP libraries.

paper.Title: A Hybrid Vectorized Merge Sort on ARM NEON

fields.Research type: Algorithm design and performance evaluation of a SIMD‑based hybrid vectorized merge sort.
fields.By process phases, stages: Design of register optimization, column sorting network, hybrid bitonic merging network, implementation, and experimental benchmarking.
fields.By technology, framework, or platform: ARM NEON SIMD intrinsics, C++ (std::sort, boost::block_sort), FT2000+ processor.
fields.By application field and/or industry dom: General sorting for data processing, databases, image and visual computing, big‑data workloads.
fields.Gaps and challenges: Minimizing register‑to‑memory traffic, limited SIMD registers, designing efficient asymmetric merging networks.
fields.Findings in research: NEON‑MS achieves 3.8× speedup over std::sort, 2.1× over boost::block_sort, and 1.25× over their parallel versions on average.
fields.Evaluation method: Empirical benchmarks on a single FT2000+ core comparing against standard and parallel sorting libraries.

paper.Title: A Load-Balanced Parallel and Distributed Sorting Algorithm Implemented with PGX.D

fields.Research type: Experimental performance evaluation of a parallel distributed sorting algorithm
fields.By process phases, stages: Data partitioning, load-balanced merging, communication latency hiding, workload balancing
fields.By technology, framework, or platform: Implemented in the PGX.D distributed graph processing framework; compared with Spark's sorting
fields.By application field and/or industry dom: Distributed big-data sorting for graph analytics and parallel supercomputing workloads
fields.Gaps and challenges: Load imbalance, communication overhead, synchronization barriers, high memory consumption limiting scalability
fields.Findings in research: PGX.D sorting achieves 2–3× speedup over Spark, efficiently handles duplicated entries, keeps workloads balanced, reduces overhead
fields.Evaluation method: Empirical runtime benchmarks on varied data distributions, comparing PGX.D implementation against Spark
